---
title: "Day 4 — Exercise: Filter FASTQ with `noodles-fastq`"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Use [`noodles-fastq`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/) to read FASTQ records, drop those shorter than a minimum length, and write the survivors back out:

```rust
fn filter_fastq<R: BufRead, W: Write>(
    reader: R,
    writer: W,
    min_len: usize,
) -> std::io::Result<(usize, usize)>
```

Return value: `(kept, total)` — how many records survived and how many we saw.

This is the first exercise that **writes** a bioinformatics file format as well as reading one.

## The crate

```toml
[dependencies]
noodles-fastq = "0.20"
```

You need:

- [`fastq::io::Reader::new(BufRead)`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/latest/noodles_fastq/io/struct.Reader.html#method.new) and its [`.records()`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/latest/noodles_fastq/io/struct.Reader.html#method.records) iterator
- [`fastq::io::Writer::new(Write)`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/latest/noodles_fastq/io/struct.Writer.html#method.new) and its [`.write_record(&record)`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/latest/noodles_fastq/io/struct.Writer.html#method.write_record)
- [`record.sequence()`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fastq/latest/noodles_fastq/record/struct.Record.html#method.sequence): the returned value has its own `.len()` method — call it directly. (`record.sequence().as_ref()` also works if you first bind it as `let seq: &[u8] = ...` so the compiler can pick the right `AsRef` impl.)

## The exercise

```bash
cd day4/ex-fastq-filter
```

Make all three tests pass. They use `Cursor<&[u8]>` for input and `Vec<u8>` for output, so no temp files.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- data/sample.fastq /tmp/filtered.fastq 5
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — the basic shape"}
```rust
let mut reader = fastq::io::Reader::new(reader);
let mut writer = fastq::io::Writer::new(writer);
let mut total = 0;
let mut kept = 0;
for result in reader.records() {
    let record = result?;
    total += 1;
    if record.sequence().len() >= min_len {
        writer.write_record(&record)?;
        kept += 1;
    }
}
Ok((kept, total))
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — why `<W: Write>` and not `W = File`"}
By taking any `Write`, we can:
- write to a file in `main` (via `File::create`),
- write to a `Vec<u8>` in tests,
- write to stdout (`std::io::stdout()`),
- gzip-encode the output by wrapping in `flate2::write::GzEncoder`,
- all without changing the function.

This is the same trade we made on day 4 exercise 2 for the reader: a tiny bit of generic syntax buys total composability.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
See `day4/ex-fastq-filter-solution/src/main.rs` for the full reference. About 15 lines.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Day 4 exercise 5**: bundle the filtered output, the stats from exercise 2, and a plot from exercise 4 into a single `.zip` report.
- **Day 5**: parallelise filtering across many input files with [`rayon`](https://docs.rs/rayon/).
